lessons.explanation: المستقبل المستمر (Future Continuous) يتكون من will be + verb-ing. نستخدمه للتحدث عن حدث سيكون مستمراً في وقت محدد في المستقبل. مثال: 'At 8 PM tonight, I will be studying.' يمكن استخدامه أيضاً للحديث عن أحداث مخططة في المستقبل أو للتوقع المهذب لما يفعله الآخرون الآن.

examples.sentence: This time next week, I will be flying to Paris.
examples.meaning: At that specific time, the action of flying will be in progress.

examples.sentence: At 9 AM tomorrow, she will be giving a presentation.
examples.meaning: The presentation will be ongoing at 9 AM.

examples.sentence: Don't call me at 6 PM; I will be having dinner.
examples.meaning: I will be in the middle of dinner at 6 PM.

examples.sentence: In 2050, scientists will be developing new energy sources.
examples.meaning: The development will be ongoing in 2050.

vocabulary.translation: في مثل هذا الوقت الأسبوع القادم

lessons.vocabulary_exercise: Fill in the blanks: 1. The construction work is ___ and will continue for months. (ongoing/scheduled) 2. I'm busy now; ___, my colleague is handling the clients. (meanwhile/by then) 3. The meeting is ___ for 3 PM. (scheduled/predicted) 4. ___ I will be lying on a beach in Thailand. (This time next week/By then)
lessons.grammar_exercise: Complete the sentences using future continuous: 1. At 10 PM tonight, I ___ (watch) a movie. 2. This time next month, we ___ (celebrate) our anniversary. 3. Don't visit me at lunchtime; I ___ (have) a meeting. 4. In five years, she ___ (work) as a doctor. 5. At 8 AM tomorrow, they ___ (travel) to the airport.
lessons.writing_task: Write a paragraph describing your typical day in 10 years. Use at least 6 future continuous sentences to describe what you will be doing at different times. Example: 'In ten years, I will be working as a software engineer. At 9 AM, I will be leading my team meeting.'
lessons.dialogue: A: What will you be doing this time next week? B: I'll be lying on a beach in Sharm El-Sheikh. A: Lucky you! I'll be sitting in an office, working on reports. B: Don't worry. This time next month, you'll be enjoying your holiday too. A: I hope so! What time will you be flying? B: At 7 AM, I'll be boarding the plane.

quiz.question: What is the structure of future continuous?
Complete: At this time tomorrow, I ___ in the ocean.
Future continuous is used for:
options: Actions in progress at a future time
quiz.answer: Actions in progress at a future time

quiz.question: Which sentence is in future continuous?
options: I will go to the store
I will be going to the store at 5 PM
I will have gone to the store
quiz.answer: I will be going to the store at 5 PM

quiz.question: What does 'This time next year, I will be living in Canada' mean?
options: I will have finished living there
I will be in the process of living there
I will visit Canada for a day
quiz.answer: I will be in the process of living there

objectives: Learn the structure of future perfect (will have + past participle)
Use future perfect to talk about completed actions by a future time
Distinguish between future continuous and future perfect
lessons.explanation: المستقبل التام (Future Perfect) يتكون من will have + past participle. نستخدمه للتحدث عن حدث سيكون قد اكتمل قبل وقت محدد في المستقبل. عادة نستخدم 'by' (بحلول) أو 'by the time' للإشارة إلى الموعد النهائي. مثلاً: 'By 2030, scientists will have found a cure.'

examples.sentence: By 2030, scientists will have found a cure for many diseases.
examples.meaning: The cure will be complete before 2030.

examples.sentence: I will have finished this project by Friday.
examples.meaning: The project will be completed before Friday.

examples.sentence: By the time you arrive, I will have left.
examples.meaning: My departure will be complete before your arrival.

examples.sentence: In two years, she will have graduated from university.
examples.meaning: Graduation will be completed in two years.

lessons.vocabulary_exercise: Choose the correct word: 1. The ___ for the project is next Monday. (deadline/milestone) 2. We need to ___ this task before the weekend. (complete/accomplish) 3. Graduating from university is a major ___ in life. (milestone/deadline) 4. Please prepare the documents ___. (beforehand/by the time)
lessons.grammar_exercise: Complete the sentences using future perfect: 1. By next year, I ___ (save) enough money for a car. 2. She ___ (finish) her degree by 2028. 3. By the time we arrive, the movie ___ (start). 4. They ___ (build) the bridge by the end of 2027. 5. I hope I ___ (complete) all my tasks by Friday.
lessons.writing_task: Write a letter to your future self 10 years from now. Use at least 5 future perfect sentences to describe what you hope to have achieved by then. Example: 'By the time you read this, you will have graduated, found a job, and travelled to at least five countries.'
lessons.dialogue: A: By the time we reach the airport, the plane will have taken off. B: We should have left earlier. A: Don't worry. By next week, we will have forgotten all about this. B: I hope so. By then, I will have booked a better flight. A: And by the end of the year, we will have visited five different countries!

quiz.question: What is the structure of future perfect?
Which preposition is commonly used with future perfect?
Future perfect expresses:
options: An action in progress at a future time
An action completed before a future time
quiz.answer: An action completed before a future time

quiz.question: Which is correct?
options: By tonight, I will finish my homework
By tonight, I will have finished my homework
By tonight, I will be finishing my homework
By tonight, I finished my homework
quiz.answer: By tonight, I will have finished my homework

objectives: Learn key scientific and technological vocabulary
Use future tenses to discuss scientific advancements
Practice making predictions about technology
lessons.explanation: في عالم العلوم والتكنولوجيا، هناك مفردات أساسية يجب إتقانها لمناقشة التطورات المستقبلية. استخدام المستقبل المستمر والمستقبل التام يساعد في التعبير عن التوقعات العلمية. سنتعلم مفردات مثل artificial intelligence, genetic engineering, renewable energy, space exploration, وغيرها.

examples.sentence: In 2050, scientists will be researching cures for cancer using AI.
By 2040, humans will have landed on Mars.
Soon, robots will be performing complex surgeries.
By 2035, we will have developed fully self-driving cars.

lessons.vocabulary_exercise: Match the term to its definition: 1. AI A. استكشاف الفضاء 2. Cybersecurity B. الذكاء الاصطناعي 3. Space exploration C. الأمن السيبراني 4. Renewable energy D. طاقة متجددة 5. Automation E. الأتمتة
lessons.grammar_exercise: Complete these predictions using future continuous or future perfect: 1. By 2050, scientists ___ (develop) fusion energy. 2. In 2040, astronauts ___ (explore) the surface of Mars. 3. By 2030, AI ___ (replace) many human jobs. 4. Soon, people ___ (use) virtual reality for everyday tasks. 5. By 2028, the first fully electric plane ___ (complete) its commercial flight.
lessons.writing_task: Write an essay about the future of technology. Make at least 8 predictions using future continuous and future perfect. Cover topics like AI, space exploration, renewable energy, and medicine. Example: 'By 2040, scientists will have cured most genetic diseases, and robots will be performing routine medical checkups.'
lessons.dialogue: A: What scientific breakthroughs do you predict by 2050? B: I think scientists will have found cures for most cancers. A: And what about space exploration? B: By 2050, astronauts will be living on Mars permanently. A: Do you think AI will have taken over most jobs? B: Some jobs, yes. But humans will still be doing creative work.

quiz.question: Complete: By 2040, we ___ (develop) fully functional quantum computers.

lessons.lesson_title: Making Predictions about the Future of Technology
objectives: Combine future continuous and future perfect in predictions
Discuss the impact of technology on society
Express opinions about future technological developments
lessons.explanation: للتحدث بطلاقة عن المستقبل، نحتاج إلى الجمع بين المستقبل المستمر (للأحداث المستمرة) والمستقبل التام (للأحداث المكتملة). يمكننا أيضاً استخدام التعبيرات التمهيدية مثل 'I predict that...', 'It is estimated that...', 'Experts believe that...', 'By the year...'.

examples.sentence: I predict that by 2050, scientists will have created fully functional artificial organs.
In the next decade, AI will be transforming every industry.
By 2035, renewable energy will have replaced fossil fuels as the main energy source.
In 20 years, students will be learning in virtual reality classrooms.

lessons.vocabulary_exercise: Fill in the blanks: 1. The discovery of penicillin was a major medical ___. (breakthrough/implementation) 2. This new technology will ___ the way we communicate. (revolutionize/disrupt) 3. There are serious ___ about genetic engineering. (ethical concerns/innovations) 4. Many traditional jobs may become ___ due to automation. (obsolete/sustainable)
lessons.grammar_exercise: Complete the predictions using the correct future form: 1. By 2030, electric cars ___ (become) more affordable than petrol cars. 2. In the next decade, smart homes ___ (use) AI to manage energy consumption. 3. By 2050, scientists ___ (find) a way to reverse aging. 4. In 20 years, people ___ (communicate) through brain-computer interfaces. 5. By 2045, humans ___ (establish) a permanent colony on the moon.
lessons.writing_task: Write a detailed prediction essay about technology in 2050. Use at least 10 future tense sentences (mix of future continuous and future perfect). Address 5 areas: medicine, transportation, communication, energy, and education. Include predictions, supporting reasons, and potential concerns.
lessons.dialogue: A: By 2040, how do you think technology will have changed our lives? B: I think AI will have revolutionized healthcare. A: What about transportation? B: By then, we will be flying in electric air taxis. Fossil fuel cars will have become obsolete. A: And education? B: Students will be learning through virtual reality, and AI tutors will have replaced traditional teachers in many subjects.

quiz.question: Complete: By 2040, AI ___ (take over) many complex tasks.
Which expression is used for predictions?

objectives: Understand the structure of causative have
Use 'have something done' to express services arranged for someone
Distinguish between doing something yourself and having it done
lessons.explanation: صيغة السببية (Causative) مع have: have + object + past participle. نستخدمها عندما نطلب من شخص آخر أن يفعل شيئاً لنا (عادة مقابل مال). مثلاً: 'I had my hair cut' يعني أن الحلاق هو من قص شعري، وليس أنا. يمكن استخدامها في جميع الأزمنة: have/has (حاضر), had (ماضي), will have (مستقبل).

examples.sentence: I had my hair cut yesterday.
examples.meaning: I paid a hairdresser to cut my hair.

examples.sentence: She is having her house painted this week.
examples.meaning: She hired painters to paint her house.

examples.sentence: We will have our car repaired next week.
examples.meaning: We will take the car to a mechanic.

examples.sentence: He has his suits cleaned at the dry cleaner's.
examples.meaning: He doesn't clean them himself.

lessons.vocabulary_exercise: Choose the correct word: 1. I need to ___ a technician to fix the computer. (hire/have) 2. The building is under ___ and will be modernised. (renovation/repair) 3. Regular ___ of your car is essential. (maintenance/service) 4. I took my watch to a ___ for repair. (professional/hire)
lessons.grammar_exercise: Rewrite the sentences using causative have: 1. A hairdresser cuts my hair every month. → I ___. 2. A mechanic fixed my car yesterday. → I ___. 3. A painter will paint my office next week. → I ___. 4. A cleaner cleans my apartment every Friday. → I ___. 5. A dentist checked my teeth last week. → I ___.
lessons.writing_task: Write a paragraph about all the services you use in your life. Use at least 6 causative have sentences in different tenses. Example: 'I have my groceries delivered every week. Last month, I had my kitchen renovated. Next year, I will have my garden redesigned.'
lessons.dialogue: A: You look different! Did you have your hair cut? B: Yes, I had it cut at the new salon. I also had my nails done. A: It looks great! I'm having my house painted next week. B: Are you doing it yourself? A: No, I'm having it done by professionals. I had my kitchen renovated last month and they did an excellent job.

quiz.question: What is the structure of causative have?
What does 'I had my car washed' mean?
Complete: She ___ her portrait painted by a famous artist.
Transform: A tailor makes my suits. → I ___.
options: have my suits made by a tailor
quiz.answer: have my suits made by a tailor

quiz.question: What tense is: 'I am having my house painted'?

objectives: Learn the structure of causative get
Use 'get something done' in informal contexts
Distinguish between causative have and causative get
lessons.explanation: صيغة السببية مع get: get + object + past participle. مشابهة لـ have لكنها أقل رسمية (informal). تستخدم في المحادثات اليومية. 'Get' قد توحي بأن المتحدث قام ببعض الجهد لتحقيق الإجراء. الفرق: have أكثر رسمية من get. مثال: 'I got my phone fixed' غير رسمي، 'I had my phone repaired' رسمي أكثر.

examples.sentence: I got my phone fixed yesterday.
examples.meaning: I arranged for someone to fix my phone.

examples.sentence: She needs to get her eyes tested.
examples.meaning: She needs to see an optician.

examples.sentence: We got our passports renewed last week.
examples.meaning: We applied and received new passports.

examples.sentence: He's getting his suit cleaned for the wedding.
examples.meaning: He's sending his suit to the cleaner's.

lessons.vocabulary_exercise: Fill in the blanks: 1. I need to ___ my computer; it's running slowly. (fix/get fixed) 2. We ___ a new air conditioner yesterday. (had installed/got installed - both work) 3. She went to the doctor for a ___. (check-up/renewal) 4. I need to ___ my passport. (renew/fix)
lessons.grammar_exercise: Rewrite using causative get (informal): 1. A technician fixed my laptop. → I ___. 2. The plumber repaired the leaky pipe. → We ___. 3. A professional will clean the carpets. → They ___. 4. The optician tested her eyes. → She ___. 5. A tailor is altering my dress. → I ___.
lessons.writing_task: Write an informal email to a friend about all the things you've gotten done recently. Use at least 6 causative get sentences. Example: 'Last week, I finally got my car fixed. I also got my teeth whitened at the dentist. Next week, I'm getting my apartment painted.'
lessons.dialogue: A: Did you get your laptop fixed? B: Yes, I got it repaired at the shop on Main Street. A: How much did it cost? B: Not much. I also got my phone screen replaced. A: I need to get my camera cleaned. B: There's a good place near here. I got my lenses cleaned there last week.

quiz.question: Which is more informal: causative have or causative get?
What does 'get something done' mean?
options: Arrange for someone else to do it
quiz.answer: Arrange for someone else to do it

quiz.question: Complete: I need to ___ my hair cut.
Transform: Someone delivers my food. → I ___.

objectives: Review passive voice across all tenses
Learn when to use passive vs active voice
Practice transforming active sentences to passive
lessons.explanation: المبني للمجهول (Passive Voice) يتكون من be + past participle. الفاعل غير مهم أو غير معروف. المبني للمجهول شائع جداً في الكتابة الأكاديمية والعلمية. نستخدمه عندما نريد التركيز على الفعل أو النتيجة وليس على من قام بالفعل. إذا أردنا ذكر الفاعل، نستخدم 'by'.

examples.active: Scientists have discovered a new species.
examples.passive: A new species has been discovered.

examples.active: The company will launch a new product.
examples.passive: A new product will be launched.

lessons.vocabulary_exercise: Match the term: 1. Active voice A. الفاعل هو من يقوم بالفعل 2. Passive voice B. يتبع by 3. Agent C. المفعول به هو محل التركيز 4. Impersonal D. غير شخصي
lessons.grammar_exercise: Rewrite these sentences in passive voice: 1. The chef prepares the meal. → ___. 2. The students completed the project. → ___. 3. Someone has stolen my wallet. → ___. 4. The company will announce the results tomorrow. → ___. 5. They are building a new hospital near my house. → ___.
lessons.writing_task: Write a short news report (at least 8 sentences) using passive voice in various tenses. Report on a fictional event (e.g., a scientific discovery, an art exhibition, or a natural disaster). Example: 'A new species of dinosaur has been discovered in Egypt. The fossils were found by a team of archaeologists.'
lessons.dialogue: A: Did you hear? A new museum is being built in the city center. B: Yes, it was designed by a famous architect. A: I heard the opening has been delayed. B: Yes, the date hasn't been announced yet. But it will be opened next year. A: Many rare artifacts will be displayed there.

quiz.question: What is the passive of 'They make coffee in Brazil'?
options: Coffee is being made in Brazil

quiz.question: When do we use passive voice?
options: When the action is more important than the agent
quiz.answer: When the action is more important than the agent

quiz.question: What is the passive of 'Shakespeare wrote Romeo and Juliet'?
options: Romeo and Juliet was written by Shakespeare
Romeo and Juliet is written by Shakespeare
Romeo and Juliet has been written by Shakespeare
quiz.answer: Romeo and Juliet was written by Shakespeare

quiz.question: Complete: The window ___ (break) by the boy.
What preposition introduces the agent in passive?

lessons.lesson_title: Arts and Media Vocabulary, Describing Services
objectives: Learn arts and media vocabulary
Use causative structures to describe services
Combine passive voice with causative in context
lessons.explanation: في مجال الفنون والإعلام، هناك مفردات محددة لوصف اللوحات والأفلام والمسرحيات والموسيقى. استخدام السببية (causative) يساعد في وصف الخدمات المتعلقة بالفنون مثل الطباعة والتأطير والتحرير. المبني للمجهول شائع جداً في النقد الفني والتقارير الإعلامية.

examples.sentence: The Mona Lisa was painted by Leonardo da Vinci. I had a print of it framed.
The film was directed by a young filmmaker. She got the script edited by a professional.
The album was recorded in London. They had the sound mixed by a famous producer.
The photos were taken by a renowned photographer. I got them printed in large format.

lessons.vocabulary_exercise: Complete the sentences: 1. The art ___ was held at the national museum. (exhibition/gallery) 2. The film received excellent ___ from critics. (reviews/broadcasts) 3. The ___ for this movie was composed by Hans Zimmer. (soundtrack/sculpture) 4. A ___ is being made about climate change. (documentary/masterpiece) 5. The news will be ___ at 8 PM. (broadcast/reviewed)
lessons.grammar_exercise: Rewrite the sentences using the structure indicated in brackets: 1. Someone edited the film professionally. (causative get) → The director ___. 2. A famous artist painted her portrait. (passive) → Her portrait ___. 3. A technician restored the old photograph. (causative have) → She ___. 4. The gallery displays the paintings. (passive) → The paintings ___. 5. A professional framed my picture. (causative get) → I ___.
lessons.writing_task: Write a review of a film, exhibition, or concert you attended or know about. Use at least 6 passive sentences and 4 causative sentences. Describe what was shown/played, who directed/organised it, and what services were used in its production.
lessons.dialogue: A: Have you been to the new art exhibition? B: Yes, I went yesterday. The paintings were created by a young Egyptian artist. A: How were they displayed? B: Very well. Each painting was lit perfectly. I got my favorite print framed at the gallery shop. A: I had my portrait painted last year. It was done by a local artist. B: That's nice! Did you have it framed too?

quiz.question: What is a 'documentary'?
options: A factual film about real events
quiz.answer: A factual film about real events

quiz.question: Complete: The film was ___ by Steven Spielberg.
Which sentence uses passive correctly?
options: The painting was painted by the artist
The painting painted by the artist
The painting is paint by the artist
The painting was paint by the artist
quiz.answer: The painting was painted by the artist

objectives: Learn linking words for addition (furthermore, moreover, in addition, also)
Learn linking words for contrast (however, nevertheless, although, despite)
Use these linking words in academic and professional writing
lessons.explanation: أدوات الربط (Linking words) ضرورية لربط الأفكار وجعل الكتابة أكثر سلاسة وترابطاً. للإضافة: furthermore, moreover, in addition, besides, what's more. للتباين: however, nevertheless, on the other hand, although, even though, despite, in spite of. لكل منها قواعد نحوية مختلفة: 'although' يتبعه جملة، بينما 'despite' يتبعه اسم أو verb-ing.

examples.sentence: The candidate has excellent qualifications. Furthermore, he has ten years of experience.
The project was difficult. However, we managed to complete it on time.
Although it was raining, we went for a walk.
Despite the heavy rain, we went for a walk.

lessons.vocabulary_exercise: Fill in the blanks with appropriate linking words: 1. The company increased profits. ___, it expanded into new markets. (Furthermore/However) 2. The plan seemed perfect. ___, there were some hidden risks. (Moreover/However) 3. ___ the challenges, the team succeeded. (Despite/Although) 4. ___ he was tired, he continued working. (Despite/Although)
lessons.grammar_exercise: Complete the sentences with the correct linking word: 1. ___ (Despite/Although) the high cost, we decided to invest. 2. The product is popular. ___, (However/Furthermore) it has some design flaws. 3. She is highly qualified. ___, (Moreover/However) she speaks three languages. 4. ___ (Despite/In spite of) working hard, he didn't get the promotion. 5. The hotel was expensive. ___, (Nevertheless/In addition) the service was excellent.
lessons.writing_task: Write a balanced argument essay (pros and cons) about whether social media is beneficial or harmful. Use at least 8 linking words for addition and contrast. Structure: Introduction, Arguments for (with addition words), Arguments against (with contrast words), Conclusion.
lessons.dialogue: A: I think technology has improved our lives significantly. Furthermore, it has made communication easier. B: However, there are negative effects too. People spend too much time on their phones. A: That's true. Nevertheless, the benefits outweigh the drawbacks. B: Although I agree to some extent, I think we need to be more mindful of technology use.

quiz.question: Complete: ___ it was expensive, I bought it.
Which word means the same as 'furthermore'?
Choose the correct sentence:
options: Despite he was sick, he went to work
Despite being sick, he went to work
Despite he is sick, he went to work
Despite his sickness, but he went to work
quiz.answer: Despite being sick, he went to work

objectives: Learn linking words for cause and effect
Use therefore, as a result, consequently, due to, because of
Distinguish between cause and effect connectors
lessons.explanation: أدوات الربط للسبب والنتيجة تساعد في شرح العلاقات المنطقية بين الأفكار. 'Because' و 'since' و 'as' تتبعها جمل كاملة وتعبر عن السبب. 'Therefore' و 'as a result' و 'consequently' تعبر عن النتيجة. 'Due to' و 'owing to' و 'because of' تتبعها أسماء أو phrases (وليس جمل كاملة).

examples.effect: Therefore, they had to reduce staff.
Consequently, the research was delayed by six months.

lessons.vocabulary_exercise: Match the connector to its function: 1. Because A. نتيجة 2. Therefore B. سبب 3. Due to C. نتيجة (رسمي) 4. Consequently D. سبب + اسم
lessons.grammar_exercise: Complete the sentences using the correct linking word: 1. ___ (Due to/Because) the economic crisis, many businesses closed. 2. The flight was delayed. ___ (As a result/Because), we missed our connection. 3. ___ (Since/Due to) the new regulations, companies must reduce emissions. 4. She didn't study. ___, (Therefore/Due to) she failed the exam. 5. The roads were flooded ___ (because of/because) the heavy rain.
lessons.writing_task: Write a cause-and-effect essay about climate change. Explain at least 5 causes and their effects using appropriate linking words. Use: due to, because, therefore, as a result, consequently, lead to. Example: 'Due to increased carbon emissions, global temperatures have risen. Consequently, polar ice caps are melting.'
lessons.dialogue: A: Why are you so tired? B: Because I didn't sleep well last night. A: Due to the noise from the construction site? B: Exactly. As a result, I couldn't focus at work today. A: You should go to bed earlier. B: I know. Therefore, I'll skip the gym tonight and rest.

quiz.question: What is the difference between 'due to' and 'because'?
Complete: He missed the bus. ___, he was late for work.
Which is correct?
options: Due to he was sick, he stayed home
Because of his sickness, he stayed home
Because his sickness, he stayed home
Owing to he was sick, he stayed home
quiz.answer: Because of his sickness, he stayed home

objectives: Learn sequencing words: firstly, secondly, finally, meanwhile, subsequently
Use sequencing to organise ideas in writing and speaking
Structure paragraphs and essays effectively
lessons.explanation: كلمات التسلسل (Sequencing words) تستخدم لتنظيم الأفكار ترتيبياً في الكتابة والكلام. 'Firstly, secondly, thirdly, finally' لترتيب النقاط. 'Meanwhile' للأحداث المتزامنة. 'Subsequently' و 'afterwards' للتسلسل الزمني. 'At first... then... in the end...' لسرد قصة أو عملية. هذه الكلمات تجعل الكتابة منظمة وسهلة المتابعة.

examples.sentence: Firstly, we need to analyse the data. Secondly, we will draw conclusions. Finally, we will present our findings.
I was cooking dinner. Meanwhile, my children were doing their homework.
He graduated from university. Subsequently, he found a job at a multinational company.
First, preheat the oven. Then, mix the ingredients. After that, bake for 30 minutes. Finally, let it cool before serving.

lessons.vocabulary_exercise: Fill in the blanks: 1. ___, we will review the budget. Then we will discuss the timeline. (Firstly/Meanwhile) 2. I waited for the bus. ___, I listened to music. (Meanwhile/Finally) 3. We finished the meeting. ___, we went to lunch. (Afterwards/Firstly) 4. The project was difficult. ___, we succeeded. (Eventually/Meanwhile)
lessons.grammar_exercise: Complete the paragraph with appropriate sequencing words: ___ (1), I woke up and had breakfast. ___ (2), I went to the gym. ___ (3) I was at the gym, my friend called me. ___ (4), I took a shower and got ready for work. ___ (5), I arrived at the office and started my day.
lessons.writing_task: Write a step-by-step guide explaining how to do something (e.g., how to apply for a passport, how to bake a cake, how to write an essay). Use at least 8 sequencing words: first, firstly, second, then, after that, meanwhile, finally, in conclusion.
lessons.dialogue: A: How do I apply for a university abroad? B: Firstly, you need to choose your preferred universities. Then, prepare your documents. A: What documents? B: Your transcripts, CV, and statement of purpose. After that, submit your application online. A: Then what? B: Meanwhile, prepare for the English proficiency test. Finally, wait for the acceptance letter.

quiz.question: Which word is used for simultaneous actions?
What is the correct order of sequencing words?
Complete: I will finish my homework. ___, I will watch TV.
Which sentence uses 'meanwhile' correctly?
options: I was cooking. Meanwhile, my husband was setting the table
Meanwhile, firstly we need to start
quiz.answer: I was cooking. Meanwhile, my husband was setting the table

lessons.lesson_title: Essay and Report Writing Using Discourse Markers
objectives: Apply all discourse markers in essay writing
Structure academic essays with introduction, body, and conclusion
Write professional reports using appropriate linking words
lessons.explanation: كتابة المقالات والتقارير الأكاديمية تتطلب استخداماً متقناً لأدوات الربط (Discourse Markers). المقالة الجيدة تحتوي على: مقدمة (Introduction) تطرح الموضوع والأطروحة، جسم المقال (Body) يحتوي على فقرات منظمة، وخاتمة (Conclusion) تلخص الأفكار. في كل قسم، تستخدم أدوات ربط مختلفة: للإضافة (furthermore, moreover)، للتباين (however, nevertheless)، للسبب والنتيجة (therefore, consequently)، وللتسلسل (firstly, finally).

examples.sentence: This essay will discuss the impact of social media on society. Firstly, it will examine the positive effects. Secondly, it will explore the negative consequences. Finally, it will offer recommendations.
On the one hand, social media connects people globally. However, it can also lead to social isolation.
Due to the addictive nature of social media, many people spend excessive time online. Consequently, their productivity decreases.
In conclusion, although social media has many benefits, its drawbacks cannot be ignored. Therefore, a balanced approach is recommended.

lessons.vocabulary_exercise: Match the term to its definition: 1. Thesis statement A. خاتمة 2. Body paragraph B. جملة الأطروحة 3. Conclusion C. فقرة جسم المقال 4. Coherence D. ترابط
lessons.grammar_exercise: Complete the essay outline with appropriate discourse markers: Introduction: This essay analyses the effects of remote work. ___ (1), it will discuss benefits for employees. ___ (2), it will examine challenges for employers. ___ (3), it will consider the environmental impact. ___ (4) it will provide recommendations for effective remote work policies.
lessons.writing_task: Write a full academic essay (300-400 words) on ONE of these topics: 1. The advantages and disadvantages of online learning 2. The role of technology in modern education 3. The importance of learning foreign languages. Use at least 12 different discourse markers including addition, contrast, cause/effect, and sequencing words.
lessons.dialogue: A: How do I write a good essay? B: Firstly, start with a strong introduction that includes a clear thesis statement. A: What about the body? B: Each paragraph should focus on one main idea. Use linking words like 'furthermore' for addition and 'however' for contrast. A: And the conclusion? B: Finally, summarise your main points and restate your thesis. Therefore, your reader will remember your key arguments.

quiz.question: What is the purpose of a thesis statement?
options: To state the main argument of the essay
quiz.answer: To state the main argument of the essay

quiz.question: Which discourse marker is best to start a conclusion?
Which is NOT a discourse marker?

levels.description: مستوى متقدم في اللغة الإنجليزية يؤهل المتعلم للتحدث بطلاقة وثقة في السياقات الأكاديمية والمهنية. يركز هذا المستوى على التفكير النقدي، والكتابة الأكاديمية، والإقناع اللغوي، والتعبير عن الفروق الدقيقة في المعنى، وتحليل البيانات والأبحاث.
can_do_statements: يمكنني فهم الأفكار الرئيسية في النصوص المعقدة والطويلة، بما في ذلك النصوص الأكاديمية والمهنية.
يمكنني التفاعل مع المتحدثين الأصليين بطلاقة وعفوية دون عناء.
يمكنني إنتاج نصوص واضحة ومفصلة حول مواضيع معقدة مع تنظيم منطقي للأفكار.
يمكنني استخدام اللغة بمرونة وفعالية للأغراض الاجتماعية والأكاديمية والمهنية.
يمكنني التعبير عن نفسي بدقة واستخدام أنماط لغوية متنوعة مع إتقان الفروق الدقيقة.

objectives: Understand the structure and use of inversion after negative adverbials
Identify common negative adverbials that trigger inversion
Construct sentences using inversion for formal emphasis
Distinguish between formal inversion and normal word order
lessons.explanation: Inversion after negative adverbials is a formal grammatical structure used to add emphasis by placing the auxiliary verb before the subject. When a sentence begins with a negative or restrictive adverbial expression, the subject and auxiliary verb are inverted. Common adverbials include 'never', 'rarely', 'seldom', 'hardly ever', 'not only...but also', 'no sooner...than', 'under no circumstances', 'at no time', 'on no account', and 'little'. Remember that inversion only occurs when the negative adverbial comes at the beginning of the sentence for emphasis. If the adverbial is in its normal mid-position, no inversion is needed. This structure is primarily used in formal writing and speeches, not in casual conversation.
examples: Never have I seen such a beautiful sunset in my entire life.
Not only did she finish the project on time, but she also exceeded all expectations.
Under no circumstances should you open this door during the exam.
Hardly had we arrived when the storm began.
lessons.vocabulary_exercise: Match each negative adverbial on the left with its correct inverted sentence on the right: (1) Never - (a) had we left than the phone rang. (2) No sooner - (b) will I trust him again. (3) Rarely - (c) does she complain about anything. (4) Not only - (d) did he win but he broke the record. Write the answers: 1-__, 2-__, 3-__, 4-__.
lessons.grammar_exercise: Rewrite the following sentences using inversion: 1. I have never experienced such kindness. → Never _________________. 2. She not only speaks French but also Italian. → Not only _________________. 3. You should not open this box under any circumstances. → Under no circumstances _________________. 4. The meeting had hardly started when the fire alarm went off. → Hardly _________________.
lessons.writing_task: Write a formal email to your university professor (150-200 words) explaining why you deserve an extension on your dissertation deadline. Use at least three examples of inversion with negative adverbials to make your argument more persuasive and formal.
lessons.dialogue:
Sarah: I can't believe we finally finished that research paper! Never have I worked so hard on anything.
Ahmed: I know what you mean. Not only did we have to collect data, but we also had to analyse every single result.
Sarah: Under no circumstances would I want to repeat that experience!
Ahmed: Rarely have I felt so relieved. Let's celebrate tonight.
Sarah: No sooner said than done! I know a great restaurant nearby.

quiz.question: Which sentence correctly uses inversion?
options: Never I have seen such a mess.
Never have I seen such a mess.
Never I seen have such a mess.
Never have seen I such a mess.
quiz.answer: Never have I seen such a mess.

quiz.question: Complete: 'Not only ___ the exam, but she got the highest score.'
Which adverbial does NOT trigger inversion?
'Under no circumstances ___ this door after midnight.'
What is the main effect of using inversion?

objectives: Understand the structure and function of cleft sentences
Distinguish between 'it-cleft' and 'wh-cleft' sentences
Use cleft sentences to highlight specific information
Apply cleft sentences in formal and academic contexts
lessons.explanation: Cleft sentences are complex sentence structures used to emphasize a particular part of a sentence by dividing it into two clauses. There are two main types: it-clefts (It is/was + emphasized element + that/who + rest of sentence) and wh-clefts (What/Where/Why + clause + is/was + emphasized element). It-clefts begin with 'it' followed by a form of 'be', then the element you want to emphasize, and finally a relative clause. Wh-clefts begin with a 'wh-' word (usually 'what'), followed by a clause with 'be', and then the focus information. These structures are particularly useful in academic writing, presentations, and formal discussions where you need to direct the reader's or listener's attention to the most important information.
examples: It was John who discovered the error in the financial report.
What I need is a reliable assistant to help with the research.
It is because of your dedication that we achieved this success.
What really matters is not how much we have, but how we use it.
lessons.vocabulary_exercise: Fill in the blanks with appropriate cleft sentence vocabulary: 1. The _____ sentence is used to _____ a particular part of the sentence. 2. There are two types: it-_____ and wh-_____. 3. Cleft sentences help to bring certain information into _____. 4. A _____-cleft sentence begins with 'what' instead of 'it'.
lessons.grammar_exercise: Convert these sentences into cleft sentences: 1. Ahmed discovered the solution. (it-cleft focusing on Ahmed) → It was _____. 2. I need more time to finish the report. (wh-cleft) → What _____. 3. The team won because of their hard work. (it-cleft focusing on the reason) → It was _____. 4. She appreciates your honesty. (wh-cleft) → What _____.
lessons.writing_task: Write a paragraph (120-150 words) describing the most important achievement in your career or academic life. Use at least two it-clefts and two wh-clefts to emphasize key points.
lessons.dialogue:
Professor: So, what do you think was the turning point in the experiment?
Student: What really made the difference was adding the catalyst at the right temperature.
Professor: And who suggested that adjustment?
Student: It was Dr. Ahmed who proposed the idea during our lab meeting.
Professor: What I find most impressive is how quickly you implemented the change.
Student: It was because of your guidance that we moved so quickly.

quiz.question: Which is a correct it-cleft sentence?
options: It is the manager who approves all expenses.
It the manager who approves all expenses.
It is the manager approves all expenses.
It who approves all expenses is the manager.
quiz.answer: It is the manager who approves all expenses.

quiz.question: 'What we need ___ more funding for research.'
What is the emphasized element in 'It was Sara who called the meeting'?
Which sentence is a wh-cleft?
options: What I remember most is the kindness of strangers.
The book that I read was fascinating.
It is important to study regularly.
quiz.answer: What I remember most is the kindness of strangers.

quiz.question: Cleft sentences are most useful in:

objectives: Understand how inversion works in conditional sentences
Form inverted conditionals without 'if'
Use inversion in third, second, and mixed conditionals
Apply inverted conditionals in formal contexts
lessons.explanation: Inversion in conditional sentences is a formal structure where 'if' is removed and the auxiliary verb is placed before the subject. This is most common in third conditionals (Had + subject + past participle), second conditionals (Were + subject + to + infinitive), and with 'should' for first conditional possibilities. For third conditionals, we use 'Had + subject + past participle' instead of 'If + subject + had + past participle'. For second conditionals expressing unreal present situations, we use 'Were + subject + to + infinitive'. For first conditional possibilities, we use 'Should + subject + base verb'. This structure is considered highly formal and is typically used in academic writing, formal speeches, and literature.
examples: Had I known about the traffic, I would have left earlier.
Were she to arrive late, we would have to reschedule the meeting.
Should you need any assistance, please do not hesitate to contact us.
Had it not been for your support, I would never have completed this project.
lessons.vocabulary_exercise: Match the inverted conditional with its meaning: 1. Had I known - (a) possible future situation. 2. Were she to call - (b) past unreal situation. 3. Should it rain - (c) present unreal situation. 4. Were I you - (d) giving advice. Answers: 1-__, 2-__, 3-__, 4-__.
lessons.grammar_exercise: Rewrite these conditionals using inversion: 1. If I had studied harder, I would have passed. → Had _____. 2. If she were to win the lottery, she would travel the world. → Were _____. 3. If you need help, just ask. → Should _____. 4. If it hadn't been for the map, we would have gotten lost. → Had it _____.
lessons.writing_task: Write a formal letter of apology to a client (150-180 words) explaining why a project was delayed. Use at least three inverted conditionals to express hypothetical situations and regret.
lessons.dialogue:
Boss: Were you to finish the report by Friday, we could present it on Monday.
Employee: Had I known about the deadline earlier, I would have started sooner.
Boss: Should you need overtime, just let me know.
Employee: Thank you. Were it not for your understanding, I would be very stressed.
Boss: Had we planned better, this wouldn't have been an issue. Let's improve next time.

quiz.question: Complete: '___ she to arrive early, she could help set up.'
'___ you have any questions, please ask.'
Inverted conditionals are primarily used in:
Which is the correct inverted third conditional?
options: Had I would have known, I came.
Had I known, I would have come.
I had known, I would have come.
Had I know, I would come.
quiz.answer: Had I known, I would have come.

objectives: Learn advanced phrases for expressing strong opinions
Use hedging language to soften arguments when needed
Structure persuasive arguments logically
Respond to counterarguments effectively
lessons.explanation: Expressing strong opinions at an advanced level requires a range of linguistic tools. You can use emphatic structures such as 'There is no doubt that...', 'I firmly believe that...', 'It is undeniable that...', and 'I am convinced that...'. For balanced arguments, use phrases like 'While it is true that...', 'On the one hand...on the other hand...', and 'Having said that...'. To acknowledge counterarguments, use 'Nevertheless', 'Nonetheless', 'Despite this', and 'Even so'. Advanced connectors like 'Furthermore', 'Moreover', 'In addition', and 'Consequently' help structure your argument logically. The key is to sound confident without being aggressive, and to acknowledge other perspectives while maintaining your position.
examples: There is no question that climate change poses the greatest threat to our generation.
While some argue that technology isolates us, I firmly believe it connects us in unprecedented ways.
It is my considered opinion that education reform should be the government's top priority.
Despite the obvious challenges, I am convinced that we can achieve sustainable development.
lessons.vocabulary_exercise: Choose the correct word: 1. His argument was so (compelling / refute) that everyone agreed. 2. She tried to (refute / tenable) the opposing view with strong evidence. 3. The position is no longer (tenable / conviction) given the new facts. 4. He spoke with (conviction / assertion) about his beliefs.
lessons.grammar_exercise: Rewrite these simple opinions using more advanced structures: 1. 'I think pollution is bad.' → It is my considered opinion that _____. 2. 'Some people disagree with me.' → While some may argue that _____, I believe _____. 3. 'We should protect the environment.' → There is no doubt that _____. 4. 'The evidence supports this.' → The evidence overwhelmingly suggests that _____.
lessons.writing_task: Write a persuasive essay (200-250 words) arguing either for or against the statement: 'Social media does more harm than good.' Use at least five advanced opinion phrases and acknowledge at least one counterargument.
lessons.dialogue:
Layla: I firmly believe that remote work is the future of employment.
Karim: That's an interesting point, but isn't there something to be said for in-person collaboration?
Layla: While I accept that face-to-face interaction has value, I am convinced that productivity increases with flexible working.
Karim: Nevertheless, many companies report that creativity suffers without physical presence.
Layla: That may be true in some cases, but the evidence overwhelmingly shows that employee satisfaction is much higher with remote options.

quiz.question: Which phrase expresses the strongest opinion?
What is the function of 'While it is true that...'?
Which phrase shows you are acknowledging another viewpoint?

objectives: Use modal verbs to make logical deductions in the present
Distinguish between degrees of certainty
Apply deductive modals in professional contexts
Form negative deductions correctly
lessons.explanation: Modal verbs of deduction are used to make logical conclusions based on evidence. 'Must' expresses strong certainty (90-100% sure), 'could' and 'might' express possibility (30-50% sure), 'may' expresses moderate possibility (40-60% sure), and 'can't' expresses impossibility (negative certainty). For example, 'He must be the manager' (I see evidence like a private office). 'She can't be serious' (the idea is impossible based on what I know). 'They might be delayed' (it's possible but not certain). In professional contexts, using the correct modal shows appropriate certainty levels and helps maintain credibility. Overusing 'must' can sound arrogant, while overusing 'might' can sound uncertain.
examples: He must be very experienced - look at how he handled that difficult client.
She can't be the new director - she looks far too young for that position.
The package might arrive by Friday, but I wouldn't guarantee it.
They could be waiting for us in the conference room.
lessons.vocabulary_exercise: Order these modals from strongest to weakest certainty: must, could, might, can't. Which expresses impossibility? Which expresses possibility? Which expresses certainty? Write a sentence for each.
lessons.grammar_exercise: Complete each sentence with the correct modal: 1. The lights are off. They ___ be home. (certain they're not) 2. He's been working for 12 hours. He ___ be exhausted. (almost certain) 3. I'm not sure where she is. She ___ be in a meeting. (possible) 4. That ___ be the right answer - it doesn't make sense. (impossible)
lessons.writing_task: You are a detective writing a report (120-150 words) about a missing person case. Use modal verbs of deduction to explain your theories about what might have happened based on the evidence found.
lessons.dialogue:
Detective: The door was locked from the inside. The thief must have had a key.
Officer: So he can't be a random stranger. He could be someone from the office.
Detective: Exactly. And the safe was open. The thief might have known the code.
Officer: That means it must be an inside job. But who could it be?
Detective: The security guard might have seen something. Let's check the footage.

quiz.question: Which modal expresses the highest certainty?
Complete: 'She ___ be at work. I just saw her car in the parking lot.'
'He ___ be serious. That's the funniest thing I've heard.' (impossible)
Which sentence shows speculation rather than deduction?
options: He must be tired after the long flight.
She might be the one who called yesterday.
They can't be lost - they have a GPS.
The train must have left already.
quiz.answer: She might be the one who called yesterday.

objectives: Form past modal structures correctly (modal + have + past participle)
Use past modals for deduction, possibility, and criticism
Distinguish between 'should have', 'could have', and 'would have'
Apply past modals in professional and personal contexts
lessons.explanation: Past modal verbs follow the structure: modal + have + past participle. 'Must have + PP' expresses certainty about a past event ('She must have left early'). 'Can't have + PP' / 'Couldn't have + PP' express impossibility ('He can't have forgotten'). 'Might have / May have / Could have + PP' express possibility ('They might have missed the train'). 'Should have + PP' expresses criticism or regret about something that didn't happen ('You should have told me'). 'Would have + PP' expresses a hypothetical result in the past ('I would have helped if you had asked'). 'Needn't have + PP' expresses an unnecessary past action ('I needn't have worried'). Note that 'could have' can also express ability that wasn't used ('I could have won but I gave up').
examples: She must have been exhausted after running that marathon.
You should have informed us before making such a big decision.
He can't have driven all the way here in just two hours.
They could have won the contract if they had offered a better price.
lessons.vocabulary_exercise: Complete the sentences with appropriate words: 1. Looking back with _____, I realize I made a mistake. 2. She expressed _____ for not attending the meeting. 3. It was a _____ opportunity that we didn't take. 4. In _____ situations, we imagine different outcomes.
lessons.grammar_exercise: Rewrite the sentences using past modals: 1. It's possible that she forgot the appointment. → She might _____. 2. I'm sure he didn't mean to offend you. → He can't _____. 3. It was wrong of you not to call. → You should _____. 4. It wasn't necessary for her to buy a new laptop. → She needn't _____.
lessons.writing_task: Write a reflective journal entry (150-200 words) about a professional mistake you made or observed. Use at least four different past modal structures to discuss what should have happened, what could have been done differently, and what must have caused the problem.
lessons.dialogue:
Mona: I can't believe we lost the client!
Omar: We should have prepared better for the presentation.
Mona: You're right. We must have underestimated their expectations.
Omar: The manager couldn't have been happy with our performance.
Mona: She might have given us another chance if we had shown more effort.
Omar: Well, I could have worked harder on the financial data. I regret that.

quiz.question: 'He ___ finished the report. I saw it on his desk.' (certainty)
Which sentence expresses criticism?
options: They might have arrived by now.
He could be the new manager.

quiz.question: 'You ___ seen her yesterday - she's in London this week.' (impossible)
What does 'needn't have done' express?
options: Something that should have been done

quiz.question: 'She ___ passed the exam. She studied very hard.' (logical conclusion)

objectives: Understand the purpose and function of hedging in professional communication
Use hedging devices to soften claims and express caution
Distinguish between appropriate certainty and uncertainty
Apply hedging in written and spoken professional contexts
lessons.explanation: Hedging is a linguistic strategy used to make statements less direct or absolute, showing caution, politeness, or uncertainty. It is essential in academic and professional contexts to avoid overstatement and to appear more objective. Common hedging devices include modal verbs (might, may, could), adverbs of frequency (sometimes, often, generally), probability adverbs (probably, possibly, presumably), introductory phrases (It seems that, It appears that, It could be argued that, There is evidence to suggest that), quantifying expressions (to some extent, in some cases, a number of), and verbs like 'tend to', 'appear to', 'seem to', 'suggest'. Over-hedging can make you sound unsure, while under-hedging can make you sound arrogant or overconfident. The key is to match the level of certainty to the strength of your evidence.
examples: It could be argued that the current policy is not achieving its intended goals.
The data seems to suggest that there is a correlation between the two variables.
This is arguably the most significant discovery in the field this decade.
To some extent, the results support our initial hypothesis.
lessons.vocabulary_exercise: Replace the bold words with more appropriate hedging language: 1. 'This is **the** solution.' → This is _____ the most effective solution. 2. '**Everyone** agrees.' → _____ people would agree. 3. 'It is **impossible**.' → It _____ to be impossible. 4. '**This proves** everything.' → This _____ everything.
lessons.grammar_exercise: Rewrite these strong statements using hedging language: 1. 'This drug cures cancer.' → There is some evidence to suggest that _____. 2. 'Students hate homework.' → Students _____ dislike homework. 3. 'The economy is failing.' → It could be argued that _____. 4. 'She is the best candidate.' → She is arguably _____.
lessons.writing_task: Write a short research summary (150-180 words) about the effects of social media on mental health. Use hedging language appropriately - the research should be presented cautiously, not as absolute fact. Use at least five hedging devices.
lessons.dialogue:
Dr. Hassan: The preliminary results seem to indicate that the new treatment is effective.
Colleague: But isn't the sample size quite small?
Dr. Hassan: You're right. It would be premature to draw firm conclusions at this stage.
Colleague: So the data tentatively suggests a positive trend?
Dr. Hassan: Precisely. It appears that further research is needed to confirm these findings.

quiz.question: What is the main purpose of hedging?
options: To make statements more cautious and objective
quiz.answer: To make statements more cautious and objective

quiz.question: Which sentence uses hedging?
options: Climate change is definitely caused by humans.
The evidence suggests that climate change may be linked to human activity.
Everyone knows climate change is real.
quiz.answer: The evidence suggests that climate change may be linked to human activity.

quiz.question: 'Arguably' is used to:
options: Present an opinion without claiming it's definitely true
quiz.answer: Present an opinion without claiming it's definitely true

quiz.question: What is the problem with over-hedging?
options: You may sound unsure or unconvincing
quiz.answer: You may sound unsure or unconvincing

objectives: Use advanced language for professional networking and meetings
Master diplomatic language for giving feedback and criticism
Use appropriate register for different professional situations
Handle difficult conversations with tact and professionalism
lessons.explanation: Professional communication at C1 level requires mastering register, tone, and diplomatic language. For giving constructive feedback, use the 'sandwich method' (positive - constructive - positive) with phrases like 'One area for development could be...' and 'It might be worth considering...'. For meetings, use 'Shall we move on to...', 'I'd like to build on what X said', and 'With all due respect, I see it differently'. For networking, use 'It's a pleasure to meet you', 'I've heard great things about your work', and active listening phrases. For handling conflict, use 'I understand your perspective, however...' and 'Perhaps we could find a middle ground'. Always match your language to your audience and the formality of the situation.
examples: With all due respect, I think we might be overlooking a key factor here.
I appreciate your input, and perhaps we could explore an alternative approach.
One area that could be strengthened is the way we handle customer complaints.
It's a pleasure to finally meet you in person - I've admired your work from afar.
lessons.vocabulary_exercise: Complete with the correct word: 1. Being _____ means you express your views confidently without being aggressive. 2. Showing _____ helps you understand colleagues' feelings. 3. Business _____ includes dressing appropriately and being punctual. 4. Building _____ with clients leads to long-term relationships.
lessons.grammar_exercise: Rewrite these direct statements more diplomatically: 1. 'You're wrong.' → I see it differently, and perhaps _____. 2. 'This report is bad.' → I think there are some areas _____. 3. 'That's a terrible idea.' → I appreciate the suggestion, though _____. 4. 'You need to work harder.' → It might be beneficial to _____.
lessons.writing_task: Write an email to a colleague (180-200 words) giving constructive feedback on a presentation they delivered. Use diplomatic language, the sandwich method, and at least three hedging devices. Be supportive but honest.
lessons.dialogue:
Manager: Thank you for your presentation, Sara. You had some excellent data analysis.
Sara: Thank you. I appreciate the feedback.
Manager: One area that might benefit from further development is the time management section. It seemed a bit rushed.
Sara: I see what you mean. I could spend more time on that part.
Manager: That being said, your visual aids were outstanding. Perhaps you could share your technique with the team?
Sara: I'd be happy to. Thank you for the constructive feedback.

quiz.question: What is the 'sandwich method' of feedback?
options: Praise, then constructive criticism, then praise
quiz.answer: Praise, then constructive criticism, then praise

quiz.question: What does 'with all due respect' signal?
Which phrase is most appropriate for networking?
options: It's a pleasure to meet you
quiz.answer: It's a pleasure to meet you

quiz.question: What is the best way to handle a disagreement in a meeting?
options: Raise your voice to be heard
Acknowledge the other view and present your perspective calmly
quiz.answer: Acknowledge the other view and present your perspective calmly

objectives: Construct complex sentences with multiple subordinate clauses
Use relative clauses, adverbial clauses, and noun clauses effectively
Maintain clarity while using complex structures
Combine different clause types in academic writing
lessons.explanation: Complex sentences contain one main clause and at least one subordinate clause. Advanced academic writing requires combining multiple clause types: relative clauses (who, which, that, whose), adverbial clauses (although, because, while, whereas, despite), and noun clauses (that, whether, what). A well-written complex sentence might contain a main clause, a relative clause, and an adverbial clause. For example: 'Although the study was limited in scope, the researchers, who had extensive experience in the field, concluded that further investigation was necessary.' Pay attention to clause order, punctuation, and ensuring the main idea remains clear despite additional information. Varying sentence structure between simple, compound, and complex sentences improves readability.
examples: The experiment, which was conducted over six months, yielded results that surprised even the lead researcher.
Although the initial findings were promising, the team acknowledged that more data was needed before drawing conclusions.
Students who study regularly, even when they feel tired, tend to perform better than those who delay their work.
What the report failed to mention, which is rather concerning, is that the sample size was too small to be statistically significant.
lessons.vocabulary_exercise: Identify the clause types in this sentence: 'The researcher (who led the study) concluded (that the hypothesis was correct) (because the data supported it).' Label each bracketed section as: relative clause, noun clause, or adverbial clause.
lessons.grammar_exercise: Combine these simple sentences into one complex sentence: 1. The study was published. It received widespread attention. It challenged existing theories. → The study, which _____, challenged existing theories. 2. The results were unexpected. The team double-checked the data. The data was accurate. → Because _____, the team double-checked _____. 3. Some critics disagree. They have not provided evidence. This weakens their argument. → What weakens the critics' argument is that _____.
lessons.writing_task: Write a paragraph (150-180 words) summarizing a scientific breakthrough. Use at least six complex sentences with a mix of relative, adverbial, and noun clauses. Ensure clarity is maintained despite the complexity.
lessons.dialogue:
Professor: Your essay has good ideas, but the sentences are quite simple.
Student: How can I improve them?
Professor: Try combining related ideas. For instance, instead of two short sentences, use a relative clause.
Student: So instead of 'The theory is new. It explains the data.' I could write 'The theory, which is new, explains the data'?
Professor: Exactly. And add adverbial clauses to show relationships between ideas.

quiz.question: What is a complex sentence?
options: A sentence with two main clauses
A sentence with one main clause and at least one subordinate clause
quiz.answer: A sentence with one main clause and at least one subordinate clause

quiz.question: Identify the relative clause: 'The book that I borrowed was fascinating.'
Which word typically introduces an adverbial clause of contrast?
'What the study reveals' is an example of:
What is the risk of using too many clauses in one sentence?
options: The sentence may become unclear or confusing
quiz.answer: The sentence may become unclear or confusing

objectives: Understand what nominalization is and why it is used in academic writing
Transform verbs and adjectives into nouns
Use nominalization to create formal academic tone
Recognize over-nominalization and maintain readability
lessons.explanation: Nominalization is the process of turning verbs or adjectives into nouns. For example, 'investigate' becomes 'investigation', 'decide' becomes 'decision', and 'precise' becomes 'precision'. Academic writing uses nominalization extensively because it allows for more abstract and concise expression, shifts focus from actions to concepts, and creates a formal, objective tone. Common suffixes include -tion/-sion (investigation, conclusion), -ment (development, assessment), -ness (effectiveness, awareness), -ity (diversity, reliability), and -ance/-ence (performance, evidence). However, over-nominalization can make writing dense and difficult to read. Good academic writers balance nominalized forms with active verbs for clarity.
examples: The investigation of the phenomenon led to the discovery of a new species. (from investigate → investigation)
There has been a significant improvement in patient outcomes since the implementation of the new protocol.
The effectiveness of the treatment was evaluated through a rigorous assessment process.
The development of renewable energy sources is essential for the reduction of carbon emissions.
lessons.vocabulary_exercise: Convert these verbs and adjectives to nouns: 1. investigate → _____ 2. decide → _____ 3. precise → _____ 4. develop → _____ 5. effective → _____ 6. assess → _____ 7. achieve → _____ 8. reliable → _____
lessons.grammar_exercise: Rewrite these sentences using nominalization: 1. 'We investigated the problem thoroughly.' → A thorough _____ was conducted. 2. 'The team decided to implement changes.' → The team made _____. 3. 'The results are accurate.' → The _____ of the results is confirmed. 4. 'They evaluated the program effectively.' → An effective _____ was carried out.
lessons.writing_task: Write an academic abstract (150-180 words) for a research paper on any topic you choose. Use at least eight nominalizations. The abstract should sound formal and objective. Include the research aim, method, findings, and conclusion.
lessons.dialogue:
Student: My professor said my writing is too informal. What should I do?
Tutor: Try using nominalization. Instead of 'We tested the samples', write 'The testing of the samples was conducted'.
Student: So I focus on the action as a concept rather than who did it?
Tutor: Exactly. It makes the writing more objective and academic. But don't overdo it - balance is key.
Student: That explains why academic texts feel so different from everyday speech.

quiz.question: What is nominalization?
options: Turning verbs or adjectives into nouns
quiz.answer: Turning verbs or adjectives into nouns

quiz.question: Which is the nominalized form of 'decide'?
Why is nominalization common in academic writing?
options: It creates a formal, objective tone
quiz.answer: It creates a formal, objective tone

quiz.question: What suffix turns 'investigate' into a noun?
What is the risk of excessive nominalization?
options: Writing becomes dense and hard to read
quiz.answer: Writing becomes dense and hard to read

objectives: Use hedging strategies appropriately in academic texts
Distinguish between strong claims and appropriately cautious claims
Apply hedging in literature reviews and discussions
Recognize disciplinary conventions for hedging
lessons.explanation: Hedging is crucial in academic writing to express caution, acknowledge limitations, and avoid overstatement. Academic claims must be supported by evidence, and hedging reflects the degree of certainty that evidence provides. Common hedging strategies include: introductory verbs (suggest, indicate, appear, seem, tend), modal verbs (may, might, could, would), adverbs of probability (probably, possibly, presumably, arguably, seemingly), adjectives (possible, likely, plausible, tentative), nouns (possibility, likelihood, tendency, suggestion), and phrases (it could be argued that, there is evidence to suggest, to a certain extent). Different disciplines have different conventions - sciences tend to hedge more cautiously, while humanities may make bolder claims. The key is to match your certainty to your evidence strength.
examples: It could be argued that the Industrial Revolution marked the beginning of modern environmental degradation.
The data appear to suggest a correlation between screen time and reduced attention spans, though further research is needed.
This is arguably one of the most significant studies conducted in this field to date.
There is a strong possibility that the observed effects are due to factors other than those measured.
lessons.vocabulary_exercise: Replace the words in bold with more cautious academic alternatives: 1. 'This **proves** that...' → This _____ that... 2. 'It **is** true that...' → It _____ to be true that... 3. '**Every** study shows...' → _____ studies show... 4. 'This is **the** cause.' → This is _____ the cause.
lessons.grammar_exercise: Rewrite these absolute claims with appropriate hedging: 1. 'Social media destroys real relationships.' → It could be argued that social media _____. 2. 'The experiment failed.' → The experiment did not _____ as expected. 3. 'All students prefer online learning.' → There is some evidence that students _____. 4. 'This is the best solution.' → This is arguably _____.
lessons.writing_task: Write a literature review paragraph (180-200 words) on the topic of 'The impact of artificial intelligence on employment'. Use at least six hedging devices. Present different viewpoints cautiously and acknowledge limitations of existing research.
lessons.dialogue:
Supervisor: Your conclusion is too absolute. You wrote 'This proves the theory'.
Student: But the data supports it.
Supervisor: It 'suggests' or 'indicates' - never 'proves'. Science is about probability, not certainty.
Student: So I should say 'The data strongly suggests that the theory is valid'?
Supervisor: Much better. And mention limitations. 'Pending further research, these findings suggest that...'

quiz.question: Why is hedging important in academic writing?
options: To express appropriate caution and avoid overstatement
quiz.answer: To express appropriate caution and avoid overstatement

quiz.question: Which verb is commonly used for hedging?
What does 'It could be argued that...' express?
options: A tentative claim that others might challenge
quiz.answer: A tentative claim that others might challenge

quiz.question: Which discipline typically uses the most hedging?
What is the problem with this sentence: 'The results prove that our hypothesis is correct'?
options: It is too absolute and not appropriately cautious
quiz.answer: It is too absolute and not appropriately cautious

objectives: Structure academic essays with clear introduction, body, and conclusion
Write effective thesis statements and topic sentences
Use cohesive devices to link paragraphs and ideas
Apply the C1-level conventions for academic essay writing
lessons.explanation: A well-structured academic essay at C1 level follows a clear framework. The introduction should include a hook to engage the reader, background context, and a clear thesis statement that presents your main argument. Each body paragraph should begin with a topic sentence that states the main idea, followed by supporting evidence (examples, data, citations), analysis of that evidence, and a concluding or linking sentence. The conclusion should restate the thesis in new words, summarize main points, and provide a final thought or implication. Advanced cohesion is achieved through discourse markers (furthermore, nevertheless, consequently, in contrast), lexical cohesion (using synonyms and related words), and referencing (this, these, such). The essay should maintain a formal register throughout with appropriate hedging and academic vocabulary.
examples: Thesis statement: 'While technology has undoubtedly transformed education, this essay will argue that traditional teaching methods remain essential for developing critical thinking skills.'
Topic sentence: 'One significant advantage of renewable energy is its long-term economic viability.'
Concluding statement: 'In light of the evidence presented, it is clear that climate change requires immediate and coordinated global action.'
Cohesive link: 'Having examined the economic arguments, this essay will now turn to the social implications of the policy.'
lessons.vocabulary_exercise: Match the essay component to its function: 1. Hook - (a) presents the main argument of the essay. 2. Thesis - (b) introduces the paragraph's main idea. 3. Topic sentence - (c) grabs the reader's attention. 4. Conclusion - (d) summarizes and provides final thoughts. Answers: 1-__, 2-__, 3-__, 4-__.
lessons.grammar_exercise: Identify the essay component in each sentence: 1. 'This essay will examine three key factors contributing to urban migration.' → _____ 2. 'Firstly, economic opportunities in cities attract workers from rural areas.' → _____ 3. 'To conclude, while urbanisation presents challenges, its benefits cannot be ignored.' → _____ 4. 'Imagine a world where more than 70% of the population lives in cities.' → _____
lessons.writing_task: Write a complete essay (350-400 words) arguing for or against the statement: 'Universities should focus exclusively on employability skills rather than traditional academic knowledge.' Include a clear introduction with thesis, three body paragraphs with topic sentences, and a conclusion.
lessons.dialogue:
Student: I always struggle with essay conclusions.
Teacher: Your conclusion should do three things: restate your thesis in new words, summarize key points, and end with a strong final thought.
Student: So not just repeating the introduction?
Teacher: Exactly. Paraphrase, don't repeat. And add something new - a recommendation, a question, or a broader implication.
Student: And each paragraph needs a topic sentence?
Teacher: Yes. It tells the reader what that paragraph is about and helps with overall coherence.

quiz.question: What is the purpose of a thesis statement?
options: To present the main argument of the essay
quiz.answer: To present the main argument of the essay

quiz.question: What should a topic sentence do?
options: Introduce the main idea of the paragraph
quiz.answer: Introduce the main idea of the paragraph

quiz.question: What should NOT be in a conclusion?
What does 'signposting' mean in essay writing?
options: Adding road signs to the essay
Guiding the reader through the structure of the essay
Writing in a very direct style
quiz.answer: Guiding the reader through the structure of the essay

objectives: Understand the form and use of the subjunctive mood in English
Use the subjunctive after verbs of suggestion, demand, and recommendation
Use the subjunctive in fixed expressions and formal contexts
Distinguish between subjunctive and indicative moods
lessons.explanation: The subjunctive mood is used to express wishes, suggestions, demands, recommendations, and hypothetical situations. In English, the present subjunctive uses the base form of the verb regardless of the subject (I suggest that he go, not 'he goes'). It is used after verbs like suggest, recommend, insist, demand, propose, request, and after expressions like 'It is essential that', 'It is important that', 'It is necessary that', and 'It is vital that'. The past subjunctive (were) is used in unreal conditionals and wishes (I wish I were there, If I were you). The subjunctive is also found in fixed expressions: 'God save the King', 'Long live the Queen', 'Be that as it may'. This mood is formal and more common in academic and professional writing than in casual speech.
examples: I suggest that he meet with the client before making a decision.
It is essential that every student submit their assignment by Friday.
The committee recommended that the policy be reviewed immediately.
If I were in your position, I would accept the offer without hesitation.
lessons.vocabulary_exercise: Choose the correct form: 1. The manager insists that the report (is / be) submitted today. 2. I wish I (was / were) more prepared for the interview. 3. It is vital that the patient (receives / receive) treatment immediately. 4. She demanded that he (apologizes / apologize) publicly.
lessons.grammar_exercise: Rewrite these sentences using the subjunctive: 1. 'I recommend you to take the course.' → I recommend that you _____. 2. 'It is important for her to attend.' → It is important that _____. 3. 'I wish I was taller.' → I wish I _____. 4. 'The law requires all citizens to pay taxes.' → The law requires that all citizens _____.
lessons.writing_task: Write a formal proposal (150-200 words) to your company's management suggesting changes to the remote work policy. Use the subjunctive mood at least five times. Use verbs like suggest, recommend, insist, and expressions like 'It is essential that'.
lessons.dialogue:
Manager: I propose that we implement a four-day working week.
Director: That's an interesting suggestion. What do others think?
Employee: I recommend that we first conduct a trial period before making it permanent.
Manager: Good point. It is essential that we gather sufficient data.
Director: I agree. I move that we vote on starting a three-month trial next quarter.

quiz.question: Which sentence uses the subjunctive correctly?
options: I suggest that he goes to the doctor.
I suggest that he go to the doctor.
I suggest that he going to the doctor.
I suggest that he went to the doctor.
quiz.answer: I suggest that he go to the doctor.

quiz.question: Complete: 'It is essential that the package ___ by Friday.'
The past subjunctive of 'be' for all subjects is:
After which verb is the subjunctive NOT typically used?

objectives: Use advanced conditional structures beyond basic if-clauses
Master 'were it not for', 'but for', and other formal conditionals
Use mixed conditionals for complex time references
Apply advanced conditionals in persuasive and academic writing
lessons.explanation: Advanced conditionals allow for more sophisticated expression of hypothetical situations. 'Were it not for' and 'But for' mean 'if it were not for' and are used to show that one factor prevented or enabled something. 'If it were not for the scholarship, I couldn't study abroad.' → 'Were it not for the scholarship, I couldn't study abroad.' Mixed conditionals combine different time references: third conditional in the if-clause (past) with second conditional in the main clause (present) to show past actions affecting present results. For example: 'If I had taken that job, I would be rich now.' Inverted conditionals (Had I known, Were she to arrive) add formality. 'Provided that', 'as long as', 'on condition that' express conditions with varying degrees of formality.
examples: Were it not for your support, I would never have completed this project.
But for the traffic jam, we would have arrived on time.
If I had listened to his advice, I wouldn't be in this difficult situation now.
The scholarship will be renewed provided that you maintain a GPA of 3.5 or higher.
lessons.vocabulary_exercise: Match the conditional with its meaning: 1. Were it not for - (a) past condition affecting present result. 2. But for - (b) formal condition meaning 'if not for'. 3. Mixed conditional - (c) usual meaning of 'if not for'. 4. Provided that - (d) a condition that must be met. Answers: 1-__, 2-__, 3-__, 4-__.
lessons.grammar_exercise: Rewrite these sentences using advanced conditionals: 1. 'If it weren't for my glasses, I couldn't read.' → Were it not _____. 2. 'If you hadn't helped me, I would be lost now.' → But for _____. 3. 'If she studies hard, she will pass.' → Provided that _____. 4. 'If I had learned Spanish, I could talk to them now.' → Had I _____.
lessons.writing_task: Write a persuasive speech (200-250 words) about a significant historical event, explaining how things might have been different 'were it not for' key individuals or circumstances. Use at least four advanced conditional structures.
lessons.dialogue:
Historian: Were it not for the invention of the printing press, the Renaissance might never have spread so rapidly.
Student: But for Gutenberg's innovation, would literacy have increased as it did?
Historian: Unlikely. Had books remained handwritten, only the wealthy would have had access.
Student: So if printing hadn't been invented, we might still be in the Middle Ages intellectually?
Historian: That's an oversimplification, but there's truth in it.

quiz.question: What does 'Were it not for' mean?
'But for the rain, the picnic would have been perfect.' This means:
options: The rain made the picnic perfect

quiz.question: Which is a mixed conditional?
options: If it rains, I will stay home.
If I had studied, I would have passed.
If I had taken the job, I would be rich now.
If she comes, we will eat.
quiz.answer: If I had taken the job, I would be rich now.

quiz.question: Which advanced conditional would you use to show regret about a past action affecting the present?

objectives: Identify and use common rhetorical devices in English
Use persuasive techniques to strengthen arguments
Apply rhetorical questions, parallelism, and tripling effectively
Analyze persuasive language in speeches and writing
lessons.explanation: Rhetorical devices are techniques used to persuade, emphasize, and engage an audience. Key devices include: rhetorical questions (questions not expecting answers, e.g., 'Who can deny the importance of education?'), parallelism (repeating similar structures, e.g., 'We came, we saw, we conquered'), the rule of three/tripling ('Government of the people, by the people, for the people'), antithesis (contrasting ideas in parallel structure, e.g., 'It was the best of times, it was the worst of times'), anaphora (repetition at the beginning of clauses, e.g., 'I have a dream'), and hyperbole (exaggeration for effect). In persuasive writing, use emotional appeal (pathos), logical appeal (logos), and credibility appeal (ethos). Understanding these devices helps both in producing persuasive texts and in critically analyzing others' arguments.
examples: Rhetorical question: 'How many more tragedies must occur before we take action?'
Tripling: 'We must fight for justice, for equality, and for freedom.'
Antithesis: 'Ask not what your country can do for you - ask what you can do for your country.'
Anaphora: 'We will not tire, we will not falter, and we will not fail.'
lessons.vocabulary_exercise: Identify the rhetorical device in each sentence: 1. 'We shall fight on the beaches, we shall fight on the landing grounds, we shall fight in the fields.' → _____ 2. 'Is this the kind of world we want to leave our children?' → _____ 3. 'One small step for man, one giant leap for mankind.' → _____ 4. 'I have a dream that... I have a dream that...' → _____
lessons.grammar_exercise: Rewrite these simple statements using rhetorical devices: 1. 'We need change.' → How much longer _____? (rhetorical question) 2. 'We work, we save, and we plan for the future.' → We work, we save, _____ (tripling - add one more element). 3. 'It is both difficult and rewarding.' → It is not a path of ease, but _____ (antithesis). 4. 'We will achieve our goals.' → We will not stop, we will not rest, _____ (anaphora).
